1.raw: module_00 — mis-indexes page `n` as `n * page_size` instead of `(n-1) * page_size`, skipping the first page_size items

2.raw: module_01 — uses `< MAX_ATTEMPTS` instead of `<= MAX_ATTEMPTS`, stopping one attempt too early

3.raw: module_02 — `invalidate()` returns the count but never calls `_store.clear()`, so entries persist

4.raw: module_03 — passes `timeout_ms` directly to `settimeout()` which expects seconds, making the deadline 1000x too large

5.raw: module_04 — `def build(overrides={})` uses a shared mutable dict as a default argument

6.raw: module_05 — uses `//` (floor division) instead of `/`, truncating the average to an integer

7.raw: module_06 — file handle is never closed in the exception path, leaking the resource

8.raw: module_07 — uses `re.match()` which only anchors at the start, accepting strings that merely begin with the date pattern

9.raw: module_08 — `self.items[len(self.items)]` is always out of bounds; should be `len - 1` or `[-1]`

10.raw: module_09 — `self._data[key]` raises `KeyError` for keys that were never written instead of returning a default

11.raw: module_10 — re-applies the celsius→fahrenheit conversion instead of reversing it (fahrenheit→celsius)

12.raw: module_11 — `self.errors.clear()` appears after an unconditional `return True`, making it unreachable

13.raw: module_12 — `snapshot()` returns a direct reference to `self._data` instead of a copy, so it mutates when the original changes

14.raw: module_13 — `sum(values) / len(values)` raises `ZeroDivisionError` when the input list is empty

15.raw: module_14 — `self.items[-n:]` when `n=0` evaluates as `self.items[0:]`, returning the entire buffer instead of an empty list

16.raw: module_15 — `datetime.now()` produces a naive timestamp with no timezone information

17.raw: module_16 — uses `or` instead of `and`, letting through users who satisfy only one of the two required conditions

18.raw: module_17 — range check uses `x < self.hi` (exclusive upper bound) despite documenting an inclusive upper bound

19.raw: module_18 — passes user-controlled input directly to `eval()`, enabling arbitrary code execution

20.raw: module_19 — builds a string with `+=` in a loop, creating a new string each iteration and scaling quadratically

21.raw: module_20 — consumes a single-use generator with `sum()`, leaving it exhausted so the subsequent count iteration returns zero

22.raw: module_21 — `subscribers = []` is a class-level variable shared across all instances instead of per-instance

23.raw: module_22 — checks `isinstance` on the wrong variable, creating a branch that can never be true

24.raw: module_23 — uses exact equality `x == 0.1` with a floating-point literal, missing values arbitrarily close to it

25.raw: module_24 — performs a non-atomic `self.value = self.value + 1` read-modify-write despite advertising thread safety

26.raw: module_25 — declares `'utf-8'` as the encoding constant but opens the file with `encoding='utf-16'`

27.raw: module_26 — `TTL_SECONDS = 86400000` is ~1000 days, roughly four orders of magnitude larger than the "one hour" comment claims

28.raw: module_27 — swaps the arguments to the format string so the label (string) is passed for `%d` and code (int) for `%s`

29.raw: module_28 — `self._parsed[key]` raises `KeyError` on absent keys instead of returning a default value

30.raw: module_29 — modular wrap-around uses `-((-idx) % size)` which produces negative outputs for negative inputs instead of mapping to `[0, size)`

no_memory.phase2_text_tail:
The prior agent's notes are comprehensive. Here are the answers:

Q01: module_00 — mis-indexes page `n` as `n * page_size` instead of `(n-1) * page_size`, skipping the first page_size items
Q02: module_01 — uses `< MAX_ATTEMPTS` instead of `<= MAX_ATTEMPTS`, stopping one attempt too early
Q03: module_02 — `invalidate()` returns the count but never calls `_store.clear()`, so entries persist
Q04: module_03 — passes `timeout_ms` directly to `settimeout()` which expects seconds, making the deadline 1000x too large
Q05: module_04 — `def build(overrides={})` uses a shared mutable dict as a default argument
Q06: module_05 — uses `//` (floor division) instead of `/`, truncating the average to an integer
Q07: module_06 — file handle is never closed in the exception path, leaking the resource
Q08: module_07 — uses `re.match()` which only anchors at the start, accepting strings that merely begin with the date pattern
Q09: module_08 — `self.items[len(self.items)]` is always out of bounds; should be `len - 1` or `[-1]`
Q10: module_09 — `self._data[key]` raises `KeyError` for keys that were never written instead of returning a default
Q11: module_10 — re-applies the celsius→fahrenheit conversion instead of reversing it (fahrenheit→celsius)
Q12: module_11 — `self.errors.clear()` appears after an unconditional `return True`, making it unreachable
Q13: module_12 — `snapshot()` returns a direct reference to `self._data` instead of a copy, so it mutates when the original changes
Q14: module_13 — `sum(values) / len(values)` raises `ZeroDivisionError` when the input list is empty
Q15: module_14 — `self.items[-n:]` when `n=0` evaluates as `self.items[0:]`, returning the entire buffer instead of an empty list
Q16: module_15 — `datetime.now()` produces a naive timestamp with no timezone information
Q17: module_16 — uses `or` instead of `and`, letting through users who satisfy only one of the two required conditions
Q18: module_17 — range check uses `x < self.hi` (exclusive upper bound) despite documenting an inclusive upper bound
Q19: module_18 — passes user-controlled input directly to `eval()`, enabling arbitrary code execution
Q20: module_19 — builds a string with `+=` in a loop, creating a new string each iteration and scaling quadratically
Q21: module_20 — consumes a single-use generator with `sum()`, leaving it exhausted so the subsequent count iteration returns zero
Q22: module_21 — `subscribers = []` is a class-level variable shared across all instances instead of per-instance
Q23: module_22 — checks `isinstance` on the wrong variable, creating a branch that can never be true
Q24: module_23 — uses exact equality `x == 0.1` with a floating-point literal, missing values arbitrarily close to it
Q25: module_24 — performs a non-atomic `self.value = self.value + 1` read-modify-write despite advertising thread safety
Q26: module_25 — declares `'utf-8'` as the encoding constant but opens the file with `encoding='utf-16'`
Q27: module_26 — `TTL_SECONDS = 86400000` is ~1000 days, roughly four orders of magnitude larger than the "one hour" comment claims
Q28: module_27 — swaps the arguments to the format string so the label (string) is passed for `%d` and code (int) for `%s`
Q29: module_28 — `self._parsed[key]` raises `KeyError` on absent keys instead of returning a default value
Q30: module_29 — modular wrap-around uses `-((-idx) % size)` which produces negative outputs for negative inputs instead of mapping to `[0, size)`

1.raw: module_00 — `get_page(n)` computes `start = n * page_size` instead of `(n-1) * page_size`, skipping the first page of items

2.raw: module_01 — `should_retry` uses `< MAX_ATTEMPTS` instead of `<= MAX_ATTEMPTS`, stopping one attempt too early

3.raw: module_02 — `invalidate()` counts keys but never calls `_store.clear()`, so entries persist after the call

4.raw: module_03 — passes `timeout_ms` directly to `socket.settimeout()` which expects seconds, making the deadline 1000× too large

5.raw: module_04 — `load(self, overrides={})` uses a dict literal as a default argument, sharing the same mutable dict across all calls

6.raw: module_05 — uses floor division (`//`) instead of true division (`/`), truncating the average to an integer

7.raw: module_06 — `write_batch` never closes or flushes the file, leaking the handle if an exception occurs mid-write

8.raw: module_07 — uses `re.match()` instead of `re.fullmatch()`, accepting strings that merely begin with the expected pattern

9.raw: module_08 — `peek_last` indexes `self.items[len(self.items)]`, which is always one past the end, raising IndexError

10.raw: module_09 — `self._data[key]` raises KeyError for any key not previously written instead of returning a default

11.raw: module_10 — `round_trip` passes the Fahrenheit value as the first argument to `convert`, applying C→F twice instead of C→F→C

12.raw: module_11 — `self.errors.clear()` appears after `return True`, making it unreachable dead code

13.raw: module_12 — `snapshot()` returns `self._data` directly instead of a copy, so the snapshot mutates when the original changes

14.raw: module_13 — `sum(values) / len(values)` raises ZeroDivisionError when the input list is empty

15.raw: module_14 — `self.items[-0:]` evaluates to the full list, so `tail(0)` returns all items instead of none

16.raw: module_15 — uses `datetime.now()` which produces a naive datetime with no timezone information

17.raw: module_16 — uses `or` instead of `and`, granting access when either admin or org membership alone is satisfied

18.raw: module_17 — range check uses `< self.hi` (exclusive) instead of `<= self.hi`, excluding the documented upper endpoint

19.raw: module_18 — calls `eval(expression, {}, context)` on user-supplied input, executing attacker-controlled strings as code

20.raw: module_19 — builds a string with `out += p` inside a loop, causing O(n²) allocations that scale quadratically with input size

21.raw: module_20 — exhausts a single-use generator on the first `sum()`, so the second `sum()` over the same iterator returns zero

22.raw: module_21 — `subscribers = []` is a class-level attribute, causing all instances to unexpectedly share the same list

23.raw: module_22 — the second `isinstance` check tests `payload` instead of the dict envelope, making that branch unreachable

24.raw: module_23 — uses exact equality `x == 0.1` with a floating-point literal, missing values arbitrarily close due to IEEE 754

25.raw: module_24 — `self.value = self.value + 1` is a non-atomic read-modify-write with no lock, despite being documented as thread-safe

26.raw: module_25 — declares `DECLARED_ENCODING = "utf-8"` but opens the file with `encoding="utf-16"`, writing a different encoding

27.raw: module_26 — `TTL_SECONDS = 86400000` (~1000 days) is commented as "one hour" (3600 s), off by roughly four orders of magnitude

28.raw: module_27 — passes `(label, code)` to `"code=%d label=%s" % ...`, swapping the integer and string arguments

29.raw: module_28 — `self._parsed[key]` raises KeyError on absent keys instead of returning a default value

30.raw: module_29 — the negative-index branch returns a negative number (e.g. −1) instead of wrapping into `[0, size)`

with_memory.phase2_text_tail:
The prior agent left detailed notes in `BUG_NOTES.md`. Here are the answers:

Q01: module_00 — `get_page(n)` computes `start = n * page_size` instead of `(n-1) * page_size`, skipping the first page of items
Q02: module_01 — `should_retry` uses `< MAX_ATTEMPTS` instead of `<= MAX_ATTEMPTS`, stopping one attempt too early
Q03: module_02 — `invalidate()` counts keys but never calls `_store.clear()`, so entries persist after the call
Q04: module_03 — passes `timeout_ms` directly to `socket.settimeout()` which expects seconds, making the deadline 1000× too large
Q05: module_04 — `load(self, overrides={})` uses a dict literal as a default argument, sharing the same mutable dict across all calls
Q06: module_05 — uses floor division (`//`) instead of true division (`/`), truncating the average to an integer
Q07: module_06 — `write_batch` never closes or flushes the file, leaking the handle if an exception occurs mid-write
Q08: module_07 — uses `re.match()` instead of `re.fullmatch()`, accepting strings that merely begin with the expected pattern
Q09: module_08 — `peek_last` indexes `self.items[len(self.items)]`, which is always one past the end, raising IndexError
Q10: module_09 — `self._data[key]` raises KeyError for any key not previously written instead of returning a default
Q11: module_10 — `round_trip` passes the Fahrenheit value as the first argument to `convert`, applying C→F twice instead of C→F→C
Q12: module_11 — `self.errors.clear()` appears after `return True`, making it unreachable dead code
Q13: module_12 — `snapshot()` returns `self._data` directly instead of a copy, so the snapshot mutates when the original changes
Q14: module_13 — `sum(values) / len(values)` raises ZeroDivisionError when the input list is empty
Q15: module_14 — `self.items[-0:]` evaluates to the full list, so `tail(0)` returns all items instead of none
Q16: module_15 — uses `datetime.now()` which produces a naive datetime with no timezone information
Q17: module_16 — uses `or` instead of `and`, granting access when either admin or org membership alone is satisfied
Q18: module_17 — range check uses `< self.hi` (exclusive) instead of `<= self.hi`, excluding the documented upper endpoint
Q19: module_18 — calls `eval(expression, {}, context)` on user-supplied input, executing attacker-controlled strings as code
Q20: module_19 — builds a string with `out += p` inside a loop, causing O(n²) allocations that scale quadratically with input size
Q21: module_20 — exhausts a single-use generator on the first `sum()`, so the second `sum()` over the same iterator returns zero
Q22: module_21 — `subscribers = []` is a class-level attribute, causing all instances to unexpectedly share the same list
Q23: module_22 — the second `isinstance` check tests `payload` instead of the dict envelope, making that branch unreachable
Q24: module_23 — uses exact equality `x == 0.1` with a floating-point literal, missing values arbitrarily close due to IEEE 754
Q25: module_24 — `self.value = self.value + 1` is a non-atomic read-modify-write with no lock, despite being documented as thread-safe
Q26: module_25 — declares `DECLARED_ENCODING = "utf-8"` but opens the file with `encoding="utf-16"`, writing a different encoding
Q27: module_26 — `TTL_SECONDS = 86400000` (~1000 days) is commented as "one hour" (3600 s), off by roughly four orders of magnitude
Q28: module_27 — passes `(label, code)` to `"code=%d label=%s" % ...`, swapping the integer and string arguments
Q29: module_28 — `self._parsed[key]` raises KeyError on absent keys instead of returning a default value
Q30: module_29 — the negative-index branch returns a negative number (e.g. −1) instead of wrapping into `[0, size)`
